paper_metadata.title: Carbon tax acceptance in a polarized society: Bridging the partisan divide over climate policy in the US
paper_metadata.authors: Aitor Marcos, José M. Barrutia, Patrick Hartmann
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: Two Prolific online panels of US adults; Study 1 n=405 Democrats/Republicans, Study 2 n=300 balanced partisans.
exact_tax_terms_used: carbon tax is a fair policy because it raises money that can be returned to taxpayers
carbon tax is a fair policy because it does not limit freedom of choice
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Survey experiments test partisan differences in carbon tax fairness perceptions and acceptance, including a revenue-recycling fairness frame.

determinants.definition: Self-identified Democrat or Republican orientation used to compare carbon tax acceptance.
determinants.mechanism: Partisan identity shapes baseline policy support and identity-consistent responses to carbon tax cues.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Study 2 carbon tax acceptance after neutral tax description and framing manipulations.
determinants.evidence_quote: Democrats reported a significantly higher carbon tax acceptance in all experimental groups.
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Democrats > Republicans in every group; control 4.25 vs 3.25; all simple effects significant.

determinants.definition: Message framing that carbon tax revenue is returned to taxpayers and supports low-income and affected groups.
determinants.mechanism: Signals fairness and Democrat-aligned redistribution, boosting Democrats but reducing Republican support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax framed as returning money via lump-sum payments to low-income groups and transitioning communities.
determinants.evidence_quote: The interaction effect between political orientation and distributional fairness framing was significant.
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: b=-0.77, 95% CI (-1.36,-0.18), P=0.011; widened partisan gap.
determinants.notes: Acceptance rose for Democrats but fell for Republicans.

exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: Not a clear positive acceptability effect; interaction was not significant (P=0.368) and support did not increase overall.
